summarization.a: The figure shows the capacitance (C) of a device as a function of gate bias (V<sub>g</sub>) at various temperatures of hydrogen sulfide (H<sub>2</sub>S) passivation ( 50 °C, 200 °C and 350 °C) along with unpassivated sample for comparison. The inset provides a closer view of the current density (J) against gate bias.
summarization.b: The figure illustrates the capacitance (C) of a device as a function of gate bias (V<sub>g</sub>) at different frequencies (1 kHz, 10 kHz, 100 kHz, 1 MHz) for the 50 °C H2S passivated sample. The inset provides a closer view of the capacitance at 100 kHz.

b.question: Does the inset show the hysteresis of all the devices, what are the values?
b.answer:
No the inset shows only the minimum hysteresis observed for   50 °C device. The hysteresis measured around Cfb at 100 kHz  is as follows, 
1. 460 mV for the unpassivated device
2. 380 mV for the 50 °C in situ H2S passivated device
3. 585 mV for the 200 °C in situ H2S passivated device
4. 410 mV  for the 350 °C in situ H2S passivated device

b.question: How is the 50 °C in situ H2S treatment with lowest hysteresis passivating the device?
b.answer: The fact that the 50 °C passivated device, which XPS showed to have no detectable S signal at the surface, displays the best electrical properties is further evidence that S is not the primary passivating element for the in situ process. It is possible to speculate that hydrogen may be passivating the InGaAs surface. One possibility is low temperature dissociative adsorption of H2S to produce H–S and H species which may bond to As at the epitaxial layer surface.

a.question: What is the minimum capacitance of the samples?
a.answer:
The minimum capacitance Cmin,  at Vg =−2 V, 
1. Unpassivated sample = 0.0028   F/m2   
2. Passivated at 350 °C = 0.0026  F/m2       
3. Passivated at 200 °C = 0.0024  F/m2     
4. Passivated at 50 °C= 0.0022 F/m2

a.question: Which device displays the sharpest transition?
a.answer: The  in situ passivated surface at 50 °C device displays the highest maximum capacitance ( accumulation Cmax) and also exhibits the sharpest transition from minimum (depletion to accumulation).
